oneLineSummary: Python variables are dynamic references to typed memory objects, evaluated at runtime.
summary: Dynamic typing allows Python variables to reference objects of any data type without explicit declaration. Type information resides in the object itself (via `PyObject`), not in the variable name.
keyTakeaways: Variables in Python are reference tags pointing to objects in heap memory.
Objects have fixed data types; variables have no type constraints.
Python performs type checking dynamically at runtime.
Use `isinstance(obj, Type)` rather than `type(obj) == Type` to support subclasses.
Type hints (`x: int`) serve as documentation and static checking metadata; they do not enforce runtime constraints.

memoryTricks.trick: Think of variables as luggage tags attached to boxes, not the boxes themselves.
isinstance IS subclass friendly; type() is strict identity.

commonErrors.error: TypeError: can only concatenate str to str

preInterviewChecklist: Knows how duck typing works in Python functions.
